text: Out of all the Marvel movies to come out
in recent years, Thunderbolts is
definitely one of them. In fact, it's
probably the one I've been looking
forward to the least. Not because I hate
it or anything, but because I feel
absolutely nothing towards it. It's the
movie equivalent of an annual dental
checkup. You know it's coming, but it's
not like you're pumped up to get started
on it or anything. And why would you be?
It's just another by the numbers teamup
movie featuring a bunch of B and Cless
characters that most people can barely
remember from films and TV shows most of
us didn't watch taken on a nebulous
threat that's poorly explained in a
series of action scenes that look like
they've been done a million times
before. Not exactly compelling stuff
but is it really as bad as we expected
or does Thunderbolt somehow managed to
pull off a surprise win? Well, let's
find out together, shall we? The movie
begins with discount Black Widow who's
feeling sad and worthless because
there's not much going on in her life
outside of her work. And if that's not a
neat encapsulation of modern feminism
frankly, I don't know what is. Anyway
after completing her latest mission to
blow up a lab in Southeast Asia, she's
sent by Discount Selena Meyer to
infiltrate a highsecurity vault in the
middle of nowhere where an enemy agent
is about to steal a bunch of classified
secrets. Now, a normal person might
think it's a bit suspicious that
discount Selena Meyer apparently knows
exactly when and where she's going to
get robbed. But well, I guess discount
Black Widow isn't a normal person. But
when she does get there, she finds John
Walker, Ghost, and Task Manager waiting
for her. It turns out they've all been
sent here to kill each other in a kind
of circular firing squad because
Discount Selena Meyer is now under
congressional investigation and has
decided to erase all evidence of her
dodgy activities, including everyone
that she employs. Once they're sealed
inside the vault, a self-destruct timer
activates to vaporize the whole place.
Not a great plan. Wow. It's lucky that
they all happen to arrive here at the
same exact time. Otherwise, this never
would have worked. It's even more lucky
that there was a 2-minute countdown to
detonation, thus giving them fair
warning and plenty of time to escape.
It's even luckier that the power source
for the sonic barrier preventing Ghost
from unlocking the doors just so happens
to be in the room with them.
Really? Wow. Everything's just coming up
thunderbolts right now, isn't it? So
they make it out just in the nick of
time, naturally. And it turns out
they've got a new friend with them. This
is Bob, a test subject for an
experimental new program to create the
sentry. Basically, Marvel's answer to
Superman. The longer Bob's conscious
the more his powers start to manifest
until he's captured by Discount Selena
Meyer and brought back to New York to be
unveiled to the public. Luckily though
Red Guardian shows up in a limo because
he overheard discount Selena Meyer's
entire plan when she was riding in his
car because I guess super villains are
happy to divulge their most intimate and
damaging secrets in front of complete
strangers. What are you [ __ ]
There's just all kinds of convenient
luck happening in this movie. So, the
gang heads to New York to save Bob, but
he goes a bit crazy and turns against
everyone. So, discount Selena Meyer
activates a kill switch. But then he
comes back to life as Angry Bob and
makes a big cloud of sadness that
swallows up New York City. But that's
okay because Discount Black Widow and
the gang go into the sadness cloud and
help Bob defeat Angry Bob by having a
good old cry. And then everything goes
back to normal and somehow they become
the new Avengers. Son of [ __ ]
I've said on various open bars that I
expected Thunderbolts to be a relatively
decent by the numbers and ultimately
forgettable action teamup movie that
would probably fail to make much of a
splash at the box office. But boy was I
wrong on that one. This movie is
actually surprisingly [ __ ] and the
biggest problems stem from the complete
tonal whiplash that it gives you. On the
one hand, there's actually a pretty
interesting attempt to explore the
impact of personal trauma, depression
failure, a loss of self-worth, and a
search for meaning and fulfillment in
life. There's hints of this with Yolena
and the rest of the gang, broadly
mirroring the trauma that haunts and
ultimately threatens to overcome Bob.
And holy [ __ ] the performance by Lewis
Pullman is by far the best part of this
entire movie. The guy is able to switch
from befuddled innocence to haunted
victim to a terrifying god-like entity
in the space of a single scene. And if
anything, I wanted to see more of this
[ __ ] The problem is the movie doesn't
have time to give this stuff the
narrative depth than it needs. And it
ignores most of the struggles of the
rest of the team altogether. Why?
because we need to make more time for
Marvel humor, protracted action scenes
pointless exposition, references to
stuff that most people didn't even
watch, and more Marvel humor. And this
brings me on to by far the biggest
problem with Thunderbolts. It's a tiny
kernel of an interesting and serious
idea buried under a mountain of all the
usual [ __ ] that blightes Marvel movies.
It's like they heard all of the memes
about the formulaic nature of their
films and instead of correcting
themselves to counter it, they decided
to lean even harder into it out of pure
[ __ ] spite. Not since She-Hulk have I
watched a Marvel project trying so
desperately hard to be funny and failing
so spectacularly. The character
development in this movie is completely
[ __ ] Ghost is a character that I
barely even remember from Ant-Man 2, so
this would have been a perfect time to
give her some actual personality and a
narrative arc. Instead, she does almost
nothing throughout the whole movie. Her
powers are nerfed whenever they'd be
inconvenient to the plot, which is most
of the time to be fair. And I was left
wondering why she's even in the film.
John Walker exists for no other reason
than to be wrong about everything and
constantly mocked and belittled by the
strong female characters around him. You
know, like basically every other Marvel
movie since Endgame, Red Guardians
still an obnoxiously loud [ __ ] proving
the point that just shouting stuff
really loud isn't a substitute for
humor. Buckyy's here because they
couldn't think of anything else to do
with his character. And as for ass
Blaster, well, all I'll say is don't get
too attached to her.
Oh no. What? What happened? And for the
life of me, I'll never understand why
Marvel keep trying to make Yolena a
thing. I mean, what exactly are we
supposed to find appealing about this
character? She's basically a shorter
dumpier, less appealing version of Black
Widow with a shitty Russian accent and
played by a disinterested Florence Pew
who practically faxes in her
performance. She is way better than
movies like this and she knows it. We're
better than you and we know it. You
know, there's a phenomenon here in my
country that we call the Scottish
cringe. A kind of inherited cultural
embarrassment that we all feel because
we all know we're a bit [ __ ] these days.
We never say it out loud and we don't
really admit it even to ourselves, but
deep down we all feel it. And the reason
I bring this up is that, well
Thunderbolts is a perfect example of
what I hereby dub the Marvel cringe.
Watching this movie, I was struck by
this weird feeling of embarrassment. Not
just for the people making slop like
this, but for me as the audience.
Watching a bunch of Asian actors dressed
up in their goofy outfits, reciting the
hackne dialogue and the lame jokes, the
physics defying action scenes and the
overdramatic villains. It's like
watching a teenager who's still playing
with action figures or a woman in her
30s still listening to Taylor Swift
songs or a middle-aged man still going
to nightclubs. All of them stunted
unable or unwilling to accept that the
world around them is changing and it's
time to move on. Now, the MCU
practically defined the cinematic
zeitgeist of the 2010s. And love it or
hate it, you can't deny the enormous
success it had. But that was a long time
ago now. Times have changed and the
zeitgeist has moved on. And movies like
Thunderbolts now feel like dated
unfashionable holdovers from a different
era, from a franchise that can't or
won't accept that it just isn't that
relevant anymore. a bottom of the barrel
kind of movie made to fill a release
slot and keep the MCU grinding forward
until the inevitable reboot comes along
to start the whole process over again
scraping together whatever crap happened
to be available and throwing it against
the wall in the vain hopes that
something anything would stick. Well, I
suspect that this one won't, making two
failures in a row for Marvel this year.
Now, it all hangs on Fantastic 4, and
honestly, I wouldn't want to put odds on
that movie succeeding either. As for
Thunderbolts, I recommend you save your
time and money and go do something
better.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
